title: Recruiter CV & Resume Guide 2026 — In-House, Agency & Talent Acquisition
metaDescription: Recruiter CV and resume guide for 2026: full-cycle vs agency vs in-house, ATS platforms (Greenhouse, Lever, Workday), time-to-fill metrics, Boolean sourcing, and 12 bullet examples for recruiter, senior recruiter, and TA lead roles.
intro: Whether you call it a recruiter CV or a recruiter resume, hiring managers scan for three things: the roles you've filled (function, seniority, volume), the platforms you've used (Greenhouse, Lever, Workday, LinkedIn Recruiter), and the business metrics you've moved (time-to-fill, pass-through rate, quality-of-hire, offer-accept rate). This guide covers responsibilities, required skills, keywords, bullet examples, and common mistakes for recruiter, senior recruiter, recruiting manager, and TA lead roles — agency and in-house.
responsibilities: Own full-cycle recruiting for a requisition portfolio: intake, sourcing, screening, interviewing, offer, and close
Build and maintain candidate pipelines through Boolean search, LinkedIn Recruiter, referrals, boot-camp partnerships, and talent communities
Partner with hiring managers on role scoping, leveling, interview loop design, and calibration
Run phone and video screens, score candidates against competency rubrics, and document decisions in the ATS
Coordinate on-site / virtual interview loops across 3–6 interviewers and manage candidate experience end-to-end
Negotiate offers, handle counter-offer scenarios, and close candidates against competing processes
Report weekly on pipeline health, funnel conversion, time-to-fill, and diversity slate metrics
Partner with compensation and People Ops on offer approvals, level-setting, and equity benchmarking
Contribute to employer-brand initiatives: careers page content, LinkedIn posts, conference attendance, referral programmes
requiredSkills: Boolean search, X-ray sourcing, and LinkedIn Recruiter (InMail templates, project management, pipeline tagging)
Fluency in at least one modern ATS: Greenhouse, Lever, Ashby, Workday Recruiting, SmartRecruiters, or Jobvite
Structured-interview methodology: competency rubrics, STAR-style scoring, interviewer training, bias mitigation
Hiring-manager partnership: intake meetings, kickoff docs, weekly updates, stakeholder management across skip levels
Pipeline and funnel reporting — pass-through rate, time-in-stage, submit-to-interview, interview-to-offer, offer-accept
Candidate experience: responsiveness SLAs, feedback delivery, rejection calls, NPS or Greenhouse survey interpretation
Offer negotiation, counter-offer handling, base + equity + bonus structuring, visa sponsorship basics
For agency recruiters: BD / new client acquisition, MPC (most placeable candidate) marketing, fee negotiation, contract drafting
salaryRange: In-house corporate recruiters typically earn $65,000–$110,000 base (US) or £35,000–£65,000 (UK), rising to $120,000–$180,000 base for senior recruiters and leads at high-growth tech companies. Agency recruiters often earn lower base ($40,000–$60,000) plus commission — top billers at contingent agencies clear $200,000+ OTE. Executive search consultants and in-house TA leaders earn $150,000–$300,000+.
careerPath: Head of Talent Acquisition / VP of Talent
body:
Recruiter CVs and resumes lose on one of three things: unquantified bullets, missing tool specificity, or ambiguity about whether you're agency, in-house corporate, or RPO. Fix those three and you'll clear most recruiter screens.

Start with scope in every bullet. Number of requisitions owned concurrently (typical in-house: 10–20; tech recruiter at scale: 20–30; agency 360-desk: 15–25), average time-to-fill in days, offer-accept rate, and diversity slate percentages. If you've never pulled these numbers from your ATS, pull them before writing — Greenhouse and Lever both expose funnel reports that give you the data you need. A bullet like 'Owned full-cycle engineering requisitions with 42-day average time-to-fill and 86% offer-accept across 28 hires in FY25' is worth five generic bullets about 'sourcing and screening'.

Name your ATS and sourcing stack explicitly. ATS filters match on exact tool names — 'Greenhouse' is not the same string as 'applicant tracking system'. If you've used Lever and Greenhouse and Workday, list all three. Same with LinkedIn Recruiter (vs generic 'LinkedIn'), SeekOut, Gem, HireSweet, or Fetcher. For agency recruiters, name your BD stack — Bullhorn, JobAdder, Vincere — and any MPC / talent mapping methodology you've used.

Separate agency, in-house, and RPO experience clearly. A hiring manager screening an in-house TA role wants to see requisition portfolio, stakeholder partnership, and employer brand work. A staffing-firm director wants to see desk size, billings, gross margin, and client-service metrics. Don't force agency bullets into an in-house frame — they read as thin. If you've done both, put the most relevant role first in your experience section and tailor the summary accordingly.

Speciality matters more than years. A tech recruiter who's placed 40 software engineers at growth-stage startups is not the same candidate as a clinical recruiter with 8 years filling RN roles at hospital systems — even if both have 'Senior Recruiter' in the title. Call out the function (engineering, product, sales, GTM, finance, clinical, legal, exec search), the seniority band (entry-to-senior IC, staff+, Director+, VP+, exec), and the industry. Recruiting managers want to see pattern match to their open reqs.

Show the leadership arc if you have it. Moved from recruiter to senior recruiter to recruiting manager? Show the headcount managed, how you trained and ramped sourcers, calibration processes you owned, and the funnel metrics you improved across the team. Recruiting leaders want to hire people who can scale a function, not just hit an individual number.

For UK-based recruiter CVs specifically: use 'CV' not 'resume' in your summary, spell 'organised' with an 's', and note any GDPR / right-to-work knowledge. UK agencies expect to see REC qualifications (if held), CIPD for in-house TA, and any specific sector desks (perm vs contract, city vs regional).

WadeCV reads each recruiter posting, identifies the specific ATS, sourcing stack, requisition type, and seniority band it's hiring for, and rewrites your CV or resume to match the posting's language — including regional spelling and UK-vs-US vocabulary conventions.
commonMistakes: Writing 'sourced candidates' without specifying the tools (LinkedIn Recruiter, SeekOut, Boolean on GitHub/Kaggle, referral engine) — recruiters are hired for tool fluency
Listing 'ATS experience' instead of naming Greenhouse, Lever, Workday, or Ashby — ATS filters match on exact names
Skipping the function-and-seniority tag (e.g. 'technical recruiter placing software engineers Staff and above') — recruiting is specialised and specificity sells
No metrics. Recruiting is one of the most quantifiable functions on a CV — time-to-fill, offer-accept rate, pass-through, diversity slate, cost-per-hire — omit these and your CV reads as junior
Blurring agency and in-house experience into one narrative — hiring managers for either side read this as confusion about your actual motion
Listing 'full-cycle recruiting' without describing scope (req portfolio size, concurrent opens, average time-in-stage)
Missing employer-brand or talent-community work for in-house applications — at senior / lead levels this is 30% of the role
No stakeholder or hiring-manager bullets for candidates applying to Senior Recruiter+ roles — partnership skill is what separates ICs from leads
interviewTips: Be ready to talk through a specific req you closed end-to-end: intake, sourcing channels, funnel numbers, stakeholder friction, offer-stage details
Bring your time-to-fill and offer-accept rate to the interview — if you don't know them, pull them from Greenhouse / Lever before the call
For agency interviews, be ready to quantify your desk: monthly billings, gross margin, deal mix (perm / contract), and client retention
Expect a role-play: a hiring manager with an unrealistic bar or a candidate counter-offer. Structured-interview methodology and a clear framework matter more than the right answer
Know the company's ATS and tech stack before the call — LinkedIn or Glassdoor tend to surface this and interviewers notice

faq.question: What should a recruiter CV include in 2026?
faq.answer: A strong recruiter CV in 2026 includes: (1) a 2–3 sentence summary naming your function focus, seniority band, and ATS stack; (2) experience bullets with scope numbers (reqs owned, time-to-fill, offer-accept rate, pass-through); (3) a skills section listing ATS platforms (Greenhouse, Lever, Workday, Ashby), sourcing tools (LinkedIn Recruiter, SeekOut, Gem), and methodology (structured interviews, competency rubrics); (4) keywords pulled directly from the posting. In-house vs agency framing should match the job you're applying to.

faq.question: How do I write a recruiter resume with no recruiting experience?
faq.answer: Target in-house Coordinator or Sourcer roles first — they're the documented entry points. Lead your CV with sales, customer success, or account management experience where you've managed a pipeline, driven a conversion funnel, or owned stakeholder relationships. Those translate directly. Mention any LinkedIn Recruiter, Boolean search, or ATS exposure (even informal). List the methodology courses you've taken — SourceCon, Greenhouse University, Lever resources, HireSweet Academy.

faq.question: What's the difference between an agency recruiter CV and an in-house recruiter CV?
faq.answer: Agency CVs lead with billings, desk size, deal count, perm vs contract mix, gross margin, and client retention. Employer names of agencies and clients matter. In-house CVs lead with req portfolio, time-to-fill, offer-accept rate, hiring-manager partnership, employer brand work, and team size (if lead). If you have both, split your experience section cleanly and tailor the summary to whichever role you're applying for.

faq.question: What keywords help a recruiter CV pass ATS screening?
faq.answer: Pull them from the posting first. The evergreen set includes: full-cycle recruiting, Boolean sourcing, LinkedIn Recruiter, ATS names (Greenhouse, Lever, Workday, Ashby, SmartRecruiters), structured interview, competency rubric, intake meeting, time-to-fill, offer-accept rate, pass-through rate, diversity slate, candidate experience, talent pipeline, employer brand, referral programme. For technical recruiters: add GitHub, Stack Overflow, Kaggle, Hacker Rank, CoderPad. For executive search: retained, contingent, BD, MPC, talent mapping.

faq.question: How long should a recruiter CV be?
faq.answer: One page for recruiters with under 6 years of experience. Two pages for senior recruiters, leads, or agency billers with long client lists. Three or more pages is only defensible for Director+ roles where you need to show team headcount, org design, and strategic initiatives. Cutting is the skill — every bullet should have a number.

faq.question: Do recruiters care about GPA or degree on a recruiter CV?
faq.answer: Most in-house recruiting roles don't require a specific degree, though hiring managers care about cognitive horsepower and communication. If your degree or GPA is strong (3.7+ from a top university), list it. If not, drop it after your first senior role. Agency recruiting is even more meritocratic — desk performance is the signal.

faq.question: Should I mention DEI or diversity slate metrics on a recruiter CV?
faq.answer: Yes, if you have specific results. Numbers like 'built 45% underrepresented pipeline slates on engineering reqs, maintained through offer stage' are high-value signals for companies with DEI hiring goals. Avoid generic 'passionate about diversity' language — it reads as filler. Focus on process (outreach channels, rubric calibration, sourcing partnerships) and outcome (slate percentage, hire percentage).

faq.question: What's the best resume format for a talent acquisition lead or Director of Talent Acquisition?
faq.answer: Reverse-chronological, two pages max. Open with a 3-sentence summary naming function mix (tech / sales / GTM), headcount led, hiring volume per year, and signature systems work (e.g. built Greenhouse from scratch, ran a 600-hire year, cut time-to-fill 38%). Experience bullets should alternate between individual hiring outcomes and team / systems leadership. Include a metrics table if you've consistently hit year-over-year hiring plan at scale.

Lead SEO + content strategy: programmatic SEO clusters, pillar / cluster architecture, technical SEO (Core Web Vitals, schema, internal linking) and editorial calendar
Manage agencies, freelancers and in-house specialists; review media plans, creative briefs and content calendars; own creative QA
Build and maintain a marketing dashboard with weekly self-serve reporting for executive, sales and product stakeholders
Forecast spend, pipeline contribution and headcount for the next 1-4 quarters; reconcile actuals to plan with the CFO / FP&A monthly
Own brand consistency across channels: tone, visual system, narrative architecture and messaging frameworks
Operate AI-driven workflows (ChatGPT, Claude, Surfer, Clay, Apollo, AirOps, Cassidy) for content production, ABM enrichment, ad-creative variants and lifecycle copy
requiredSkills: Multi-channel campaign ownership across paid (Meta, LinkedIn, Google Ads), organic (SEO, content), lifecycle (email, in-app, push) and partner
Marketing automation fluency with at least one enterprise platform (HubSpot Marketing Hub Enterprise, Marketo Engage, Salesforce Marketing Cloud, Pardot / Account Engagement)
Analytics and attribution with GA4, server-side GTM, Looker Studio / Looker / Mode / Hex; SQL or warehouse-native dashboarding (BigQuery / Snowflake + dbt) is increasingly required
Demand gen funnel mechanics: ICP definition, lead scoring, MQL/SQL/SQO criteria, lifecycle stage definitions, MMM and incrementality literacy
Budget and forecast management: blended CAC, payback period, channel-mix optimisation, agency $ managed, marketing budget as % of ARR
Experimentation and CRO: hypothesis design, test sizing, confidence intervals, holdout vs incrementality, winning-test rollout discipline
Stakeholder management across Sales, RevOps, Product, Design, Engineering and Finance; weekly self-serve reporting habit
Content + brand: editorial calendar, narrative architecture, voice / tone framework, programmatic SEO at scale
AI workflow design — name the tool stack, the automation, and the FTE-equivalent productivity gain
Project management at multi-campaign cadence (Asana, Jira, Linear, Notion, Monday) with cross-functional gating
salaryRange: US: $85,000–$110,000 IC manager early career; $110,000–$145,000 mid-level B2B SaaS; $145,000–$180,000+ Senior Manager / Group Manager at well-funded SaaS; +$25,000–$60,000 in NYC, SF, LA. UK: £55,000–£80,000 manager; £80,000–£110,000+ Senior Manager at SaaS scale-ups; LSE-listed and US-HQ subsidiaries pay 15-25% premium. Equity: 0.05-0.25% at Series A/B; 0.02-0.10% at Series C+.
careerPath: Director of Marketing / Head of Demand Gen
body:
Marketing manager screening in 2026 is precise. Recruiters at well-funded SaaS and DTC teams filter on (a) named platform tier (HubSpot Pro vs Enterprise, Marketo Standard vs Select), (b) marketing-sourced or marketing-influenced pipeline $, (c) blended CAC and CAC payback rather than channel CTR, and (d) AI-workflow fluency by named workflow rather than tool list. The CV that wins this screen leads with marketing-sourced or marketing-influenced ARR contribution, names the warehouse-native stack (GA4 + server-side GTM + BigQuery + Looker / Mode), and reads as a function-level operator rather than a channel specialist.

Tailor the CV to the marketing track. Demand gen / B2B SaaS: lead with MQL → SQL → SQO conversion, marketing-sourced pipeline $, blended CAC, CAC payback, and the named lead-scoring + ABM stack (HubSpot lead scoring, 6sense / Demandbase, Clay + Apollo enrichment, LinkedIn Matched Audiences). Performance / growth: lead with quarterly spend, ROAS / iROAS, channel mix, conversion API + server-side tracking, and incrementality testing. Lifecycle / CRM: lead with NRR or repeat-purchase rate, trial-to-paid conversion, and the lifecycle platform (Customer.io, Iterable, Braze, Klaviyo, Marketo Journey Builder). Content / SEO: lead with non-brand organic sessions delta, programmatic-page count, conversion or pipeline from organic, and the framework (Next.js + JSON content + ISR for N programmatic pages). Brand / category: lead with reach, share of voice, brand-aided awareness movement, earned-media placements and influenced pipeline.

The 2026 differentiator is AI-workflow fluency. List the workflow, the tool, and the FTE-equivalent productivity gain — 'Built a Claude + Surfer + Webflow content engine producing 40 long-form pages monthly (≈1.5 FTE equivalent), generating 14K incremental organic sessions and 38 SQLs/quarter at $0 incremental ad spend' is a Senior Manager+ signal. Pair the CV with a measurable side project (Substack, podcast, Indie Hackers launch) and a named conference talk if you have one — they read as load-bearing operator signals at Director+.

For the full 2026 playbook by track, level and metric stack — including the 6 bullet formulas that pass screen — see /digital-marketing-resume.
commonMistakes: Bullets that say 'managed campaigns' or 'ran ads' without channel, spend, ROAS / CAC or pipeline outcome — the screen filters those before a human reads them
No named platforms — 'marketing automation tools' instead of 'HubSpot Marketing Hub Enterprise + Marketo Engage' is invisible to boolean recruiter searches
Quoting MQL volume without SQL or SQO conversion — recruiters read MQL-only resumes as candidates who do not own the handoff to Sales
ROAS quoted without channel mix or attribution model — '8× ROAS' on last-click Meta + brand search is not the same as 8× iROAS in a geo-holdout test
AI fluency listed as 'familiar with ChatGPT' — name the workflow you have shipped (content engine, ABM enrichment, ad-creative variants) and the FTE-equivalent productivity gain
Missing the warehouse-native stack — GA4 + GTM + BigQuery + dbt + Looker + Segment + Hightouch is the 2026 expected baseline at mid-market and enterprise SaaS
Director / VP resume framed at channel-specialist level (CTR, open rate) rather than function-level (marketing-sourced ARR, pipeline coverage, headcount, marketing P&L)
Generic positioning ('results-driven marketer') instead of role-targeted (Senior Performance Marketer, $4M quarterly spend, 4.6× ROAS, $2,910 blended CAC, B2B SaaS DACH/UK)
interviewTips: Prepare 3 case studies in the channel + volume + outcome format with confidence-interval / sample-size context where you have it
Be ready to walk the marketing P&L, channel mix and quarterly forecast on a whiteboard — Director+ interviews almost always include a forecast model exercise
Bring a teardown of the company's current marketing motion: GA4 / Wappalyzer / SimilarWeb / Ahrefs analysis of their stack, organic profile and paid presence
Have a clear point-of-view on incrementality vs MMM vs MTA — Senior+ interviews increasingly include an attribution case discussion
Prepare an AI-workflow story with the FTE-equivalent productivity gain and the dollar-cost saved — it is the 2026 differentiator at every level

faq.question: What metrics should a marketing manager lead a CV with in 2026?
faq.answer: It depends on the track. Demand gen / B2B SaaS: marketing-sourced pipeline $, MQL → SQL → SQO conversion, blended CAC, CAC payback. Performance / growth: quarterly spend, ROAS / iROAS, blended CAC, channel mix, conversion API + server-side coverage, incrementality test results. Lifecycle / CRM: NRR or repeat-purchase rate, trial-to-paid conversion, revenue per send, lifecycle platform. Content / SEO: non-brand organic sessions delta, programmatic-page count, conversion or pipeline from organic. Brand / category: reach, share of voice, brand-aided awareness movement, earned media, influenced pipeline. Always pair the number with the time window and a baseline so the screener can interpret it.

faq.question: Which platforms and tools should a marketing manager list on a CV?
faq.answer: List every platform you have used in production, with the tier and modules where you can. The high-frequency 2026 recruiter search terms: GA4, server-side Google Tag Manager, HubSpot Marketing Hub (Pro / Enterprise), Salesforce Marketing Cloud, Marketo Engage, Pardot / Account Engagement, Customer.io, Iterable, Braze, Klaviyo, Segment, RudderStack, Hightouch, Census, Meta Ads Manager + Conversion API, LinkedIn Campaign Manager, Google Ads (Performance Max, Demand Gen), Ahrefs, Semrush, Screaming Frog, Surfer, Webflow, Looker Studio / Looker / Mode / Hex, Mixpanel, Amplitude, Optimizely, VWO, Statsig, Eppo, dbt and Snowflake / BigQuery. For AI: name workflows, not familiarity (Clay-driven enrichment, Surfer-driven SEO content, AirOps content pipeline, Cassidy ABM agent).

faq.question: How do I write a marketing manager CV bullet that passes ATS and recruiter screens?
faq.answer: Use the channel + volume + outcome pattern: open with a strong verb (Owned, Scaled, Shipped, Lifted, Reduced, Built), state the channel and unit of work (paid spend, organic sessions, sends, experiments), state the volume ($1.4M quarterly Meta budget, 412 SQLs, 32 experiments, 84K monthly organic sessions), and close with the business outcome (4.6× ROAS, $2,910 CAC, $5.2M influenced pipeline, +$840K ARR run-rate). Avoid 'managed', 'helped', 'assisted' as opening verbs — they read as filler. Include at least one named platform per role so the bullet survives boolean recruiter search.

faq.question: What's the difference between a Marketing Manager, Senior Marketing Manager and Director of Marketing CV?
faq.answer: Marketing Manager: channel or segment ownership, monthly campaign cadence, $200K-$1M quarterly budget, ROAS / CTR / open rate / conversion as headline metrics, 6-12 monthly campaigns shipped. Senior Marketing Manager: multi-channel ownership, blended CAC, CAC payback, MQL → SQL conversion, agency $ managed, mentoring 1-3 ICs. Director of Marketing: function-wide ownership, marketing-sourced ARR, pipeline coverage ratio, marketing budget as % of ARR (8-25%), headcount 5-25, marketing P&L, board-level metric ownership, RevOps + Sales SLA ownership. Mismatching the framing gets the CV filtered into the wrong pipeline.

faq.question: How should I show AI fluency on a marketing manager CV without sounding generic?
faq.answer: Name the workflow, the tool stack, and the FTE-equivalent productivity gain or dollar impact. Weak: 'Familiar with ChatGPT and Jasper.' Strong: 'Built a Claude + Surfer + Webflow content engine producing 40 SEO-optimised long-form pages monthly (≈1.5 FTE equivalent), generating 14K incremental organic sessions and 38 SQLs/quarter at $0 incremental ad spend.' For ABM: 'Operated a Clay + Apollo + GPT-4 enrichment pipeline qualifying 1,800 net-new accounts/month against the ICP at 22% reply-rate on outbound — replaced ~$11K/month in outsourced research.' AI fluency is now the 2026 sophistication marker.

faq.question: Should I include side projects on a marketing manager CV?
faq.answer: Yes if they are measurable and recent. A Substack with subscriber count, a podcast with download numbers, an Indie Hackers / Product Hunt launch with revenue, a published case study on a major industry blog (HubSpot, MarketingProfs, Reforge, Demand Curve), or a conference talk (SaaStr, MAU, Lifecycle Marketing Summit) all read as load-bearing operator signals. Skip vanity-only projects without metrics — they read as filler at Manager+ levels. For Director+ roles, include analyst-relations work (Gartner / Forrester briefings), board-level metric ownership and IPO-readiness work.

faq.question: How do I tailor a marketing CV for B2B SaaS vs DTC vs B2C?
faq.answer: B2B SaaS: lead with marketing-sourced ARR, MQL → SQL → SQO, blended CAC, CAC payback, ABM mechanics, RevOps alignment, named CRM (Salesforce, HubSpot). DTC ecommerce: lead with ROAS, blended CAC, repeat-purchase rate, AOV, LTV, named DTC stack (Shopify, Klaviyo, Triple Whale, Northbeam, Polar, Meta CAPI Gateway, Recharge). B2C consumer: lead with reach, brand-aided awareness movement, share of voice, earned media, app installs / DAU / WAU if mobile. The metric stack and the named tools differ enough that a generic 'multi-channel marketing manager' framing reads as under-positioned at any of the three.

title: Accountant CV & Resume Guide 2026 — Skills, Keywords & Summary Examples
metaDescription: Accountant CV and resume guide with 35+ ATS keywords, 8 FAQs, responsibilities, skills, salary bands and summary examples for staff, senior, tax, audit and corporate accounting roles.
intro: Accountants are essential to every industry — managing financial records, closing the books, ensuring compliance, and advising on budgets and controls. Whether you're targeting public accounting (Big 4 audit or tax), corporate FP&A, industry senior accountant, controller, or a government / non-profit role, your CV needs to prove technical depth (GAAP/IFRS, ERP, advanced Excel), quantified volume (accounts reconciled, returns filed, close cycle days), and measurable business impact. This guide covers the exact responsibilities, keywords, FAQs and summary templates hiring managers and ATS systems scan for in 2026.
responsibilities: Prepare and analyse financial statements (income statement, balance sheet, cash flow statement) under GAAP or IFRS
Manage the general ledger: post journal entries, accruals, prepayments, and recurring entries; reconcile intercompany balances
Own month-end, quarter-end and year-end close: run the close calendar, prepare balance sheet reconciliations, and ensure variance analyses are complete before reporting deadlines
Reconcile bank statements, accounts payable (AP), accounts receivable (AR), fixed assets, and credit card subledgers to the general ledger
Ensure compliance with GAAP, IFRS, SOX, ASC 606 revenue recognition, ASC 842 leases, and applicable local / state / federal tax regulations
Prepare and file federal, state and local tax returns (1120, 1065, 1040, 990, VAT/GST) and estimated payments; respond to tax authority enquiries
Support external audits and internal SOX testing by gathering documentation, preparing PBC schedules, and responding to auditor sample requests
Prepare budgets, rolling forecasts and variance analyses (actual vs budget vs forecast) for department managers and senior leadership
Implement and maintain internal controls, segregation of duties, and approval workflows within the ERP
Participate in ERP implementations, migrations (e.g. QuickBooks → NetSuite, Oracle → SAP S/4HANA), and finance transformation projects
Mentor junior accountants and review their journal entries, reconciliations and close deliverables for accuracy and completeness
requiredSkills: US GAAP and/or IFRS accounting standards with working knowledge of ASC 606 (revenue), ASC 842 (leases) and ASC 350/360 (impairment)
Full-cycle accounting: AP, AR, GL, fixed assets, payroll journals, intercompany and consolidations
Tax preparation and compliance: federal / state / local, plus VAT/GST for UK and EU roles
ERP fluency: SAP S/4HANA, Oracle Financials, NetSuite, Workday Financials, Microsoft Dynamics 365, Sage Intacct, QuickBooks Online / Desktop
Reconciliation and close-management tools: BlackLine, FloQast, Trintech Cadency, Workiva
Advanced Excel: pivot tables, SUMIFS, INDEX/MATCH, XLOOKUP, Power Query, financial modelling, three-statement models, variance bridges
Reporting and BI: Power BI, Tableau, Workday Adaptive Planning, Anaplan, Hyperion (HFM / Essbase / Smart View)
Data wrangling for finance: Alteryx, SQL basics for pulling transaction-level data, Python/Pandas (a plus for senior roles)
Attention to detail, accuracy, and the ability to hit recurring monthly / quarterly deadlines without compromising controls
Communication: translating accounting positions, variances and audit findings for non-finance stakeholders (engineering, ops, sales leadership)
salaryRange: US staff accountant $58,000–$78,000; senior accountant $80,000–$110,000; accounting manager $110,000–$145,000; controller $150,000–$220,000. UK: staff £32,000–£42,000; senior £48,000–£65,000; financial controller £80,000–£130,000. CPA / ACCA / ACA certification typically adds 10–20% over non-qualified peers, and Big 4 experience is portable to a corporate premium.
body:
Accountant CVs and resumes live or die on three things: certifications (CPA, ACCA, ACA, CMA — with state or body and year), quantified scope (accounts reconciled, returns filed, revenue recognised, entities consolidated), and process / control impact (close cycle days cut, audit findings resolved, material weaknesses remediated, systems implemented). Lead every bullet with a number and an outcome — not a task list. A resume that reads 'prepared journal entries and performed reconciliations' signals junior scope; a resume that reads 'reduced month-end close from 12 to 6 business days by automating 24 recurring entries in NetSuite and reconciling 180 GL accounts in BlackLine' signals a senior accountant who hiring managers will interview.

Tailor the CV to the specific accounting discipline. A **tax accountant** resume foregrounds return volume (1040s, 1120s, 1065s, sales-and-use, VAT/GST), tax-authority correspondence, and software like CCH Axcess, Thomson Reuters UltraTax, or Vertex. A **public / audit** CV for Big 4 (Deloitte, PwC, EY, KPMG) or mid-tier (BDO, Grant Thornton, RSM) should emphasise engagements led, hours realised, risk assessment (PCAOB / ISA standards), and testing methodology. A **corporate / industry** accountant's resume prioritises ERP, close automation (BlackLine, FloQast), flux / variance analysis, and partnership with FP&A. A **government / non-profit** CV shifts to GASB, fund accounting, grant reporting, and Form 990. Use the exact vocabulary of the job description — ATS keyword matchers weight 'GAAP' and 'ASC 606' as distinct terms; listing one does not score the other.

For the **summary block**, a one-sentence headline followed by 2-3 lines of metric-bearing context out-performs a generic 'detail-oriented accountant' opener. Example: 'CPA-eligible senior accountant with 6 years of month-end close ownership across multi-entity SaaS (ASC 606) and manufacturing (cost accounting). Cut close cycle from 10 to 5 business days at [Company] by redesigning the balance-sheet recon process in BlackLine; led SOX 404 walkthroughs across O2C, P2P, and R2R; QuickBooks → NetSuite migration lead for a $40M-revenue carve-out.'

Common 2026 evolutions to flag: (1) AI-assisted close tools (FloQast Flow, BlackLine Journals AI, Workday Illuminate) — hiring managers want candidates who can audit AI output, not just run it; (2) ESG / sustainability reporting (CSRD in EU, SEC climate rules) is creating demand for accountants who can map carbon and social data to financial controls; (3) Remote close increased scope — mentioning close ownership 'across three time zones' signals remote-ready; (4) ERP consolidation (QuickBooks → NetSuite, NetSuite → SAP) is the biggest single transformation project accountants will touch in 2026 — naming the migration explicitly demonstrates project scope. WadeCV ingests the job description from the URL and rewrites your accounting bullets in the exact ATS vocabulary the employer uses, so the right skills and quantified results land in the top 30% of the resume every time.
commonMistakes: Listing ERP or accounting software as a skill without context — say what you did with it (e.g. 'led NetSuite migration for a 5-entity group; reduced close by 4 days' beats 'NetSuite')
Omitting CPA / ACCA / ACA / CMA status and jurisdiction — state 'CPA (NY, 2022)' or 'ACCA affiliate (Jan 2025)' not just 'CPA candidate'
Writing generic task bullets ('managed accounts', 'prepared journal entries') without volume, entities, close days, or dollar scope
Hiding the numbers — senior accountants who can't quantify their scope look like staff accountants; always lead with counts, dollars or timelines
Missing the close-cycle metric — the single most-scanned data point for industry accounting roles is close days; state it
No SOX / internal controls language for US public-company roles — recruiters filter for SOX 404 vocabulary even on senior-accountant JDs
Using 'bookkeeping' language for senior roles — 'posted journal entries' reads junior; 'owned the accrual and variance review for a $120M P&L' reads senior
Burying industry context — a SaaS accountant with ASC 606 experience is materially more valuable to SaaS hiring managers than one without; surface it in the summary, not page two
interviewTips: Be ready to walk through a recent balance-sheet reconciliation end-to-end: what the account was, what the reconciling items were, and how you resolved them
Prepare 2-3 specific examples of errors you caught, process improvements you drove, or controls you designed — with dollar scope and time-saved
Know the company's industry and ERP before the call — accounting nuance differs sharply between SaaS (ASC 606), manufacturing (cost accounting), and non-profit (fund accounting)
Expect technical-accounting questions: deferred revenue, lease accounting (ASC 842), intercompany eliminations, foreign currency translation — have concise on-the-job examples
For senior roles, prepare a 30-60-90 day plan focused on the close calendar, control gaps, and ERP / reporting improvements you'd prioritise

faq.question: Do I need a CPA to get an accounting job?
faq.answer: No — many staff, senior, and even accounting-manager roles in industry don't require a CPA. However, a CPA (or ACCA / ACA in the UK, CMA for management accounting) meaningfully expands your options into public accounting, audit, controller and VP Finance tracks, and typically adds a 10-20% salary premium. If you're CPA-eligible (150 credit hours) or a CPA candidate, say so explicitly on the resume with the jurisdiction and target exam date.

faq.question: What accounting skills are most important for a 2026 resume?
faq.answer: Three tiers: (1) Foundational — GAAP/IFRS, full-cycle accounting, advanced Excel, ERP (NetSuite or SAP). (2) Differentiating — SOX 404 controls, ASC 606 revenue recognition, ASC 842 leases, BlackLine / FloQast close automation, Power BI or Tableau. (3) Emerging — AI-assisted close tools (FloQast Flow, BlackLine Journals AI), ESG / CSRD reporting, SQL or Alteryx for transaction-level analysis. List tier 1 and 2 in your skills section; weave tier 3 into the body of a recent bullet to signal modernity.

faq.question: How do I quantify accountant responsibilities on a CV?
faq.answer: Every bullet needs a number. Use one of: volume (180 GL accounts, 200 tax returns, $40M revenue, 5 entities), timing (close cycle 12→6 days, audit closed 3 weeks ahead of schedule), accuracy (zero penalties, clean audit two years running, 0.01% error rate), or savings (8 hours per close, $400K in recoverable discrepancies identified). If you can't get to exact numbers for confidentiality, use percentages and ranges ('reduced close by ~40%').

faq.question: What's the difference between a public accounting and corporate accountant resume?
faq.answer: Public (Big 4, mid-tier) CVs foreground engagements, hours realised, risk assessment methodology, and testing approach (walkthroughs, controls testing, substantive procedures). Corporate / industry CVs foreground close calendar ownership, ERP, variance analysis, and cross-functional partnership with FP&A and the business. If you're making the switch from public to industry, rewrite Big 4 engagements as 'led the audit for a $300M SaaS client — identified a $1.2M revenue-recognition cut-off error under ASC 606' rather than 'performed substantive testing'.

faq.question: Which ATS keywords should I include for an accounting role?
faq.answer: Read the job description and mirror the exact terms used. The most commonly-scanned terms across 2026 postings: GAAP, IFRS, month-end close, general ledger, reconciliation, SOX, ASC 606, ASC 842, journal entries, variance analysis, NetSuite or SAP (whichever is named), Excel, CPA. Include jurisdiction-specific terms (VAT for UK roles, sales-and-use tax for US roles). Don't keyword-stuff — every term should appear in a concrete bullet or skill statement, not a standalone keyword list at the bottom.

faq.question: How long should an accountant resume be?
faq.answer: One page for fewer than 8 years of experience or an early-career candidate; two pages for senior accountants, managers, controllers and CFO-track roles. In the UK a 2-page CV is standard regardless of seniority. Put the close-cycle metric, certifications, ERP list, and most-recent role above the fold — most ATS summary views and recruiter scans only read the top third.

faq.question: How do I write an accountant summary or objective?
faq.answer: Skip the generic 'detail-oriented accountant' opener. Use the formula: [Certification status] [seniority] with [years] of [specialty] across [industry]. [One headline metric with dollar / time scope]. [One differentiator — ERP migration, SOX, ASC 606, team lead, industry depth]. Example: 'CPA (IL, 2023) senior accountant with 5 years of close ownership across multi-entity SaaS. Cut close cycle from 10 to 5 business days at [Company] by redesigning recon in BlackLine; led the QuickBooks → NetSuite migration for a $60M carve-out; ASC 606 revenue-recognition subject-matter expert.'

faq.question: What accounting roles are hiring most in 2026?
faq.answer: Highest demand clusters: (1) technical accounting / revenue (ASC 606) for SaaS and subscription businesses, (2) senior accountants and close owners (every industry, driven by remote-close headcount pressure), (3) SOX controls and internal audit for newly-public companies, (4) FP&A-adjacent senior accountants who can own variance analysis and partner with the business, (5) tax accountants with international VAT / GST experience. Roles with falling demand: pure data-entry bookkeeping without systems or analytical scope.

body:
A customer service representative resume in 2026 is screened against a four-part bar: volume + quality + tooling + channel mix. Bullets that say 'helped customers' without numbers are filtered out before a human reads them. Bullets like 'Resolved 78 contacts/day across phone, Zendesk live chat and email at 4.7/5 CSAT and 86% FCR over 14 months' are screening passes.

The metric stack moves with role level. Tier 1 agents are screened on contacts/day, CSAT, FCR, AHT and QA score. Tier 2 specialists add complex-case ownership, escalation rate, and KCS authoring. Team leads are screened on team CSAT, team FCR, schedule adherence and attrition. Customer Success Managers (the alternate track) are screened on NRR, GRR, logo churn, and book size in $ARR. Match the metric stack to the level of the role you are applying for.

Vocabulary differs by industry. B2C retail support uses 'order issue', 'returns flow', 'refund SLA', 'fulfilment partner'; B2B SaaS support uses 'ticket', 'macro', 'KCS article', 'engineering triage', 'voice of customer'; fintech support uses 'dispute', 'chargeback', 'KYC', 'fraud-flag escalation'; BPO / contact-centre work uses 'queue', 'shrinkage', 'WFM', 'occupancy'. Pick the vocabulary that matches the job you are applying for, even within customer service.

Named platform experience is the single highest-leverage signal in 2026. Recruiters search candidate databases with boolean queries ('Zendesk' AND 'Salesforce', 'Gainsight' AND 'ChurnZero', 'Intercom Fin' AND 'Ada'). Resumes that list categories ('helpdesk software', 'CRM') instead of named tools are invisible to those searches. List every platform you have used in production, with modules where you can ('Zendesk Support, Guide, Talk and Explore' beats 'Zendesk').

WadeCV takes your existing customer service CV, extracts the platform stack and metric expectations from the target job URL, and rewrites your bullets with the volume + quality + impact pattern, the right tool names, and the regional spelling that matches the posting. The first fit-analysis run is free with the credit included on signup. See our broader 🔗 [customer service resume guide](/customer-service-resume) for role-by-role metrics, six bullet formulas and the platform vocabulary recruiters search for.
commonMistakes: Bullets that open with 'helped', 'assisted' or 'supported' — they read as filler and get filtered out
Generic 'CRM' or 'helpdesk software' instead of named tools (Zendesk, Salesforce Service Cloud, Intercom, Gorgias)
CSAT score quoted without sample size or time window — 4.9/5 over 12 surveys is not the same as 4.9/5 over 12,000
AHT improvement quoted without quality context — reads as rushed cases without a CSAT pair
Missing channel mix — phone-only, chat-only, omnichannel, async and BPO are different roles with different vocabulary
No metrics in the professional summary — recruiters skim the summary first; volume / CSAT / FCR / NPS belong there

Have specific numbers ready: top contact-driver themes you have seen, your highest CSAT month with sample size, your largest macro / KCS article impact in % of repeat-contact reduction

faq.question: What skills should I highlight on a customer service resume?
faq.answer: Open with named platforms (Zendesk, Salesforce Service Cloud, Intercom Fin AI, Freshdesk, Gorgias) and quantified metrics (CSAT 4.5+/5, FCR 80%+, QA score 90%+, contact volume per day). Add channel range (phone, chat, email, social, async), KCS v6 / macro-authoring fluency, QA-tool literacy (Klaus, MaestroQA, Stella Connect), and de-escalation experience. For Senior or Lead roles, add coaching, schedule adherence, attrition and side-by-side training. For CSM-track roles, add NRR, GRR, logo churn, book size in $ARR, Gainsight / ChurnZero / Totango.

faq.question: What is a good CSAT score to put on a customer service resume?
faq.answer: 4.5/5 is the baseline screening signal in mature contact centres; 4.7+ is top-quartile. Always pair with sample size and time window: '4.7/5 CSAT over 14 months on 1,200+ surveyed contacts/month' is a screening pass; '4.9/5 CSAT' with no scope reads as cherry-picked. If your environment uses NPS rather than CSAT, lead with the NPS score and movement (e.g. +12 points YoY).

faq.question: How do I write a customer service resume bullet that gets read?
faq.answer: Use the volume + quality + impact pattern: open with a strong verb (Resolved, Owned, Lifted, Reduced, Authored, Led), state the volume (78 contacts/day, 14,000 quarterly tickets), state the quality (4.7/5 CSAT, 86% FCR, 92% QA score), and close with a business outcome (28% AHT reduction, $1.27M ARR retained, 9-point NPS lift). Include at least one named tool per role (Zendesk, Salesforce, Intercom Fin, Gainsight) so the bullet survives boolean keyword searches. Avoid 'helped', 'assisted', 'supported' as opening verbs — they read as filler.

faq.question: Which CRM and helpdesk tools should I list on a customer service CV?
faq.answer: List every named platform you have used in production, with modules where you can. The high-frequency 2026 recruiter searches are: Zendesk (Support, Guide, Talk, Explore), Salesforce Service Cloud + Lightning console + Omni-Channel, Intercom (Inbox, Articles, Resolution Bot, Fin AI), Freshdesk / Freshchat, HubSpot Service Hub, Gorgias, Front, Help Scout, Kustomer, ServiceNow CSM, Genesys Cloud, NICE CXone and Five9. faq.question: What is the difference between a customer service and a customer success resume?
faq.answer: Customer service resumes lead with reactive volume metrics: contacts/day, CSAT, FCR, AHT, QA score, schedule adherence. The screener wants to know you can hold the queue at quality. Customer success resumes lead with retention and expansion metrics: NRR, GRR, logo churn, book size in $ARR, account count. The screener wants to know you can renew and expand a book. Vocabulary differs: CS uses 'queue', 'ticket', 'macro', 'escalation'; CSM uses 'QBR', 'EBR', 'success plan', 'multi-thread', 'health score'.

faq.question: How do I tailor a customer service resume for a specific industry (B2C retail, SaaS, fintech, healthcare)?

Reorder the platforms and metrics on your CV so the first 6 seconds of skim hit the industry-specific signals first.

title: Administrative Assistant CV & Resume Guide 2026 — Tasks, Summary & Keywords
metaDescription: Administrative assistant CV and resume guide for 2026: tasks for resume, professional summary examples, keywords (Microsoft Office, Google Workspace, calendar management), 12 bullet examples, and ATS tips for admin assistant, office coordinator, and senior admin roles.
intro: An administrative assistant CV — or resume, in the US — has to prove three things in six seconds: the scope of support you've owned (how many executives, how many employees in the office, how many meetings per week), the tools you're fluent in (Microsoft Office with specific modules, Google Workspace, Concur, SAP, Coupa, Slack), and the process improvements you've driven. This guide covers tasks for your resume, summary examples, keywords that pass ATS screens, 12 bullet examples for admin assistant through senior admin, and the common mistakes that keep candidates on page 7 of recruiter searches.
responsibilities: Manage executive and team calendars across time zones; schedule internal and external meetings; resolve calendar conflicts and coordinate rescheduling
Coordinate domestic and international travel: flights, hotels, ground transport, visas; manage executive travel profiles in Concur, TripActions, or Egencia
Handle inbound and outbound correspondence across email, phone, mail, and courier; draft replies on behalf of executives where appropriate
Prepare documents, decks, spreadsheets, and reports using Microsoft Word, Excel (including pivot tables and VLOOKUP), PowerPoint, and Outlook
Organize and maintain digital and physical filing systems; ensure records retention and confidentiality compliance
Own office operations: supplies procurement, vendor management, facilities coordination, and mailroom / courier workflow
Coordinate on-site and virtual events: team off-sites, client lunches, board meetings, all-hands, holiday parties; manage catering, AV, logistics, and attendee lists
Take meeting minutes, distribute action items, track follow-through, and manage shared team drives
Process expense reports in Concur, Expensify, SAP Concur, or Ramp; reconcile corporate cards and submit for approval
Support new-hire onboarding: equipment setup, access provisioning, orientation scheduling, welcome logistics
Serve as the first point of contact for visitors, vendors, and external callers — reception-level professionalism plus gatekeeping for senior leaders
requiredSkills: Microsoft Office Suite — Word (mail merge, track changes), Excel (pivot tables, VLOOKUP, basic formulas), PowerPoint (template design), Outlook (rules, shared calendars)
Google Workspace — Gmail, Google Calendar, Google Drive, Docs, Sheets, Slides, Meet
Calendar management across multiple time zones; experience with Calendly, Chili Piper, or x.ai scheduling assistants
Written and verbal communication at an executive level — clear, concise, diplomatic, and confidential
Organizational skills and attention to detail — able to juggle 10+ concurrent priorities without dropping any
Discretion and confidentiality when handling sensitive information (compensation, HR, board material, M&A)
Basic bookkeeping and expense tracking — Concur, Expensify, Ramp, Brex, or similar
Travel coordination tools — Concur Travel, TripActions / Navan, Egencia, or direct booking with airline / hotel portals
Collaboration tools — Slack, Microsoft Teams, Zoom, Webex, Google Meet
Project and document tools — Asana, Trello, Notion, Confluence, Monday.com
salaryRange: Entry-level administrative assistants typically earn $38,000–$52,000 in the US (£22,000–£30,000 UK). Senior admin assistants and office coordinators earn $50,000–$72,000 ($32,000–£42,000 UK). Executive assistants supporting C-suite earn $75,000–$130,000+ ($45,000–£80,000+ UK), with chief-of-staff adjacent EAs at top tech / finance firms clearing $150,000.
body:
Administrative assistants lose most resume screens on three things: no scope numbers, no tool specificity, and no process improvements. Fix those three and you'll clear almost every admin-assistant ATS.

Lead with scope in your professional summary and in every experience bullet. How many executives did you support (1 C-suite vs 3 VPs vs a whole office)? How many employees were in the office you managed? How many meetings per week did you schedule? How many travel bookings per month? How many events per year, and at what headcount? A summary that says 'Supported 2 C-suite leaders and a 120-employee office; managed 40+ weekly meetings and 60+ annual events' is dramatically stronger than 'organized and detail-oriented administrative professional'.

Specify every tool by name and module. 'Microsoft Office' is dead keyword weight — 'Word (mail merge, track changes), Excel (pivot tables, VLOOKUP), PowerPoint' reads as a candidate who actually uses the software. Same with Google Workspace — list the individual apps. Same with expense platforms — 'Concur' or 'Expensify' or 'Ramp' beats 'expense software'. Name the collaboration tools (Slack vs Microsoft Teams) and any travel tools (Concur Travel vs TripActions / Navan vs Egencia). ATS filters match on exact names.

Quantify process improvements. If you built a new onboarding checklist, write 'cut new-hire setup time from 3 days to 4 hours by building an automated Asana template and pre-stocking equipment lockers'. If you renegotiated a supplies vendor, write 'reduced office-supplies spend 22% ($38K annually) by renegotiating the Staples contract and consolidating orders'. These bullets separate senior admin candidates from junior ones — they show you're a force multiplier, not just a task-completer.

The professional summary / resume objective is where candidates waste the most space. Instead of 'Detail-oriented administrative assistant seeking a challenging role', write three specific sentences: (1) years of experience and primary level supported (e.g. '6 years supporting VP+ leaders in tech and professional services'), (2) core tool stack ('Microsoft Office, Google Workspace, Concur, Slack, Asana'), (3) headline outcome ('scaled an office of 40 to 180 as office manager without adding admin headcount'). That's what hiring managers read.

Administrative assistant tasks for resume entries should be written in the voice of the recipient — the hiring manager — not the candidate. Start every bullet with a verb. Include scope, tool, and outcome. Replace 'responsible for calendar management' with 'managed Outlook calendars for 3 VPs, resolving 15+ weekly conflicts across domestic and international travel schedules'.

For UK CVs specifically: spell it 'organised', 'prioritised', 'programme'; use 'CV' not 'resume' in the header; list any CIPD or reception-qualifications if held; and note right-to-work status if relevant. US resumes use 'organized', 'prioritized', 'program', 'resume'.

WadeCV reads each administrative assistant job posting, identifies the exact tools, support level (1 exec vs team vs whole office), industry (tech, legal, healthcare, finance), and regional conventions (US resume vs UK CV), and rewrites your summary, bullets, and skills section to match.
commonMistakes: Writing a generic summary ('detail-oriented administrative professional') instead of naming level supported, tool stack, and headline outcome
Listing 'Microsoft Office' without specifying modules (Word mail merge, Excel pivot tables, PowerPoint templates) — advanced module fluency is what separates senior admin candidates
Omitting scope numbers — how many executives, employees, meetings, travel bookings, events — admin work is quantifiable and recruiters expect numbers
Vague task bullets like 'assisted with office tasks' or 'handled administrative duties' — rewrite as scope + tool + outcome
Missing expense / travel tool names (Concur, Expensify, TripActions, Egencia) — ATS filters on exact platform names
No process-improvement bullets — hiring managers want to see you made something better, not just completed tasks
Mismatching regional spelling — 'organized' on a UK CV or 'organised' on a US resume signals you didn't read the posting
Burying tool names in paragraphs instead of putting them in a scannable Skills section that an ATS can parse
interviewTips: Prepare a specific example of handling competing priorities from two senior stakeholders — hiring managers ask this almost every loop
Be ready to discuss a process improvement you owned end-to-end: what was broken, what you changed, what the measurable outcome was
Know the company's tool stack before the interview — Concur vs Expensify, Slack vs Teams, Outlook vs Google Calendar — and have an opinion on each
Expect a discretion / confidentiality scenario: how you'd handle overhearing a sensitive conversation or a leaked offer letter. Structured answers beat instinctive ones
Bring a one-page reference list — admin and EA roles still rely heavily on reference checks at the offer stage

faq.question: What should an administrative assistant CV include in 2026?
faq.answer: A strong administrative assistant CV in 2026 includes: (1) a professional summary naming level of support (C-suite, VP, team, office), years of experience, and tool stack; (2) experience bullets with scope numbers (number of executives, meetings, travel bookings, events); (3) a detailed skills section listing Microsoft Office modules (Word mail merge, Excel pivot tables, PowerPoint), Google Workspace apps, expense platforms (Concur, Expensify), collaboration tools (Slack, Teams, Zoom), and any project tools (Asana, Notion); (4) process-improvement bullets; (5) keywords pulled directly from the posting.

faq.question: What is the difference between an administrative assistant and an executive assistant?
faq.answer: Administrative assistants typically support an office, department, or team with general office management and scheduling. Executive assistants support specific senior leaders (VP, SVP, C-suite) with more strategic, high-stakes work — complex multi-time-zone calendars, board material prep, gatekeeping, confidential projects, and acting as an extension of the executive. EA roles typically require 5+ years of admin experience and pay 40–100% more than comparable admin roles.

faq.question: What are good administrative assistant tasks for a resume?
faq.answer: The strongest task bullets follow 'action verb + scope + tool + outcome'. Examples: 'Managed Outlook calendars for 3 VPs, resolving 15+ weekly conflicts across domestic and international travel.' 'Processed 80+ monthly expense reports in Concur, reducing reimbursement cycle time from 9 to 4 days.' 'Coordinated 12 annual company events (50–300 attendees) within a $240K budget, with 92% employee satisfaction.' Always lead with a number.

faq.question: How do I write a strong administrative assistant summary?
faq.answer: A strong summary is three sentences: (1) years of experience and level supported ('7 years supporting C-suite and VP leaders at tech and professional services companies'), (2) core tool stack ('Microsoft Office, Google Workspace, Concur, Slack, Asana'), (3) headline outcome ('scaled a 60-person office to 200 as office manager without adding admin headcount; built onboarding system that cut new-hire setup from 3 days to 4 hours'). Avoid generic adjectives — 'detail-oriented' is filler.

faq.question: What keywords help an administrative assistant CV pass ATS screening?
faq.answer: Pull them from the posting first. The evergreen set includes: calendar management, scheduling, travel coordination, meeting coordination, meeting minutes, expense reports, Concur, Expensify, Microsoft Office (and modules), Google Workspace, office management, vendor management, event planning, onboarding support, confidentiality, Slack, Microsoft Teams, Zoom, Asana, Notion. For legal / finance admin: add 'NDA drafting', 'DocuSign', 'court filings', or 'deal support'. For medical admin: add 'HIPAA', 'EMR', 'patient scheduling'.

faq.question: How long should an administrative assistant resume be?
faq.answer: One page for candidates with under 10 years of experience. Two pages only if you have 10+ years and multiple distinct phases (office coordinator → EA → office manager). Three pages is almost never appropriate for admin roles — hiring managers screen hundreds of resumes and one tight page with quantified bullets beats a longer, vague document.

faq.question: What's the difference between an administrative assistant CV and an administrative assistant resume?
faq.answer: Mostly the name and regional spelling. UK employers say 'CV', spell 'organised' and 'programme', and often expect a one-page document with right-to-work status noted. US employers say 'resume', spell 'organized' and 'program', and don't expect work-authorization status on the document. Content and structure are otherwise identical. Match the terminology in the job posting.

faq.question: Do I need a cover letter for an administrative assistant application?
faq.answer: For in-house corporate admin roles: yes, often expected, especially at law firms, professional services, and executive-support tracks. For temp / contract admin roles placed through staffing agencies (Randstad, Adecco, Robert Half): usually skipped — the agency handles the introduction. Either way, a tailored cover letter that names the executive you'd support and the specific scope of the role is a strong signal.

body:
Digital marketing manager screening at well-funded SaaS and DTC in 2026 is heavily boolean-search-driven. Recruiters filter on (a) named platform tier (HubSpot Pro vs Enterprise, Marketo Standard vs Select, Meta CAPI vs CAPI Gateway), (b) attribution sophistication (last-click vs iROAS vs MMM), (c) blended CAC and CAC payback rather than single-channel CTR, and (d) AI-workflow fluency by named workflow rather than tool list. Resumes that omit the warehouse-native analytics stack (GA4 + server-side GTM + BigQuery + dbt + Looker / Mode) get filtered out of mid-market and enterprise screens.

Lead the CV with the headline metric stack: 'Owns $X quarterly paid spend across Meta + LinkedIn + Google Ads at Y× ROAS and $Z blended CAC; delivered M MQLs / N SQLs / P opps and $X.YM marketing-sourced pipeline against quarterly target.' Then layer in the named platform stack and the AI-workflow productivity story. Specify the marketing motion (PLG / SLG / hybrid / DTC) — the same headline numbers read differently against each.

The 2026 differentiator is incrementality + AI fluency. iROAS measured via geo holdout, MMM lift, or PSI testing separates senior performance marketers from channel specialists. AI workflow named by tool + FTE-equivalent productivity gain ('Built a Claude + Surfer + Webflow content engine producing 40 long-form pages monthly ≈1.5 FTE equivalent, generating 14K incremental organic sessions and 38 SQLs/quarter at $0 incremental ad spend') separates Senior Manager+ from IC.

For the full 2026 playbook by track, level and metric stack — including the 6 bullet formulas that pass screen — see /digital-marketing-resume.
commonMistakes: Bullets that say 'managed campaigns', 'ran ads', 'oversaw social media' without channel, spend, ROAS / CAC or pipeline outcome — the screen filters those before a human reads them
ROAS quoted without channel mix or attribution model — '8× ROAS' on last-click Meta + brand search is not the same as 8× iROAS in a geo-holdout test
Quoting MQL volume without SQL or SQO conversion — recruiters read MQL-only resumes as candidates who do not own the handoff to Sales
Missing the warehouse-native stack — GA4 + server-side GTM + BigQuery + dbt + Looker + Segment + Hightouch is the 2026 expected baseline at mid-market and enterprise SaaS
AI fluency listed as 'familiar with ChatGPT' — name the workflow you have shipped (content engine, ABM enrichment, ad-creative variants, lifecycle copy) and the FTE-equivalent productivity gain

Generic positioning ('digital marketing manager with X years of experience') instead of role-targeted (Senior Performance Marketer, $4M quarterly spend, 4.6× ROAS, $2,910 blended CAC, B2B SaaS DACH/UK)
Overweight on listing channels (10+ channels in core skills) and underweight on platform tier and named modules — recruiter searches return tier-and-module hits, not channel keyword hits
Missing budget size, team size and agency $ managed — Director+ screening looks for these three numbers in the first 30 seconds
interviewTips: Prepare 3 case studies in the channel + volume + outcome format with confidence-interval / sample-size context where you have it
Be ready to walk the marketing P&L, channel mix and quarterly forecast on a whiteboard — Director+ interviews almost always include a forecast model exercise
Bring a teardown of the company's current marketing motion: GA4 / Wappalyzer / SimilarWeb / Ahrefs analysis of their stack, organic profile and paid presence
Have a clear point-of-view on incrementality vs MMM vs MTA — Senior+ interviews increasingly include an attribution case discussion
Prepare an AI-workflow story with the FTE-equivalent productivity gain and the dollar-cost saved — it is the 2026 differentiator at every level
Be ready to discuss the warehouse-native marketing stack — GA4 + GTM + BigQuery + dbt + Looker + Segment + Hightouch — and where you would invest in tracking maturity first

faq.question: What skills do hiring managers look for in digital marketing managers in 2026?
faq.answer: Three things in priority order: (1) named platform tier and modules — HubSpot Marketing Hub Enterprise + Marketo Engage + Salesforce Marketing Cloud Journey Builder, GA4 + server-side GTM + BigQuery, Meta Conversion API + LinkedIn Conversion API + Performance Max; (2) attribution sophistication — iROAS via geo holdout / MMM / PSI rather than last-click ROAS; (3) AI-workflow fluency by named workflow with FTE-equivalent productivity gain. Channel mix and tactical campaign experience are baseline expectations at Manager level — they are no longer the differentiator.

faq.question: Which platforms and tools should a digital marketing manager list on a CV?
faq.answer: GA4 (with BigQuery export), server-side GTM, HubSpot Marketing Hub (Pro / Enterprise), Salesforce Marketing Cloud, Marketo Engage, Pardot / Account Engagement, Customer.io, Iterable, Braze, Klaviyo, Segment, RudderStack, Hightouch, Census, Meta Ads Manager + Conversion API, LinkedIn Campaign Manager + Matched Audiences, Google Ads (Performance Max, Demand Gen), DV360, The Trade Desk, TikTok Ads, Ahrefs, Semrush, Surfer, Webflow, Looker Studio / Looker / Mode / Hex, Mixpanel, Amplitude, Optimizely, VWO, Statsig, Eppo, Mutiny, Intellimize, dbt, Snowflake, BigQuery. For DTC: add Triple Whale, Northbeam, Polar, Recharge, Shopify. For ABM: add 6sense, Demandbase, RollWorks, Clay, Apollo. Recruiters filter on tier and modules — list them.

faq.question: What's the difference between a Digital Marketing Manager and Performance Marketing Manager CV?
faq.answer: Digital Marketing Manager: multi-channel ownership across paid + organic + lifecycle + content. Headline metric stack = blended CAC + CAC payback + marketing-sourced pipeline. Performance Marketing Manager: paid-acquisition focus across Meta + LinkedIn + Google + programmatic. Headline metric stack = quarterly spend + ROAS / iROAS + CAC by channel + incrementality test results. Performance Marketing leads with attribution sophistication (iROAS, MMM, geo holdouts); Digital Marketing leads with full-funnel ownership and pipeline contribution. Mismatching the framing routes the CV to the wrong pipeline.

faq.question: How do I show iROAS, MMM and incrementality on a digital marketing CV?
faq.answer: Lead with the test design and the lift result: 'Ran 6-market geo-holdout incrementality test on Meta + LinkedIn paid spend over 8 weeks; measured 38% incremental contribution against last-click attribution that had reported 100% — re-allocated $420K/quarter from over-attributed brand search to under-attributed lower-funnel paid social.' Or for MMM: 'Operated quarterly MMM (Recast / Robyn / in-house) covering $4.6M paid spend; identified 22% over-investment in Performance Max and 18% under-investment in podcast / OOH; reallocation lifted blended ROAS 17% over two quarters.' iROAS / MMM / geo-holdout are the 2026 sophistication marker for Senior+ performance roles.

faq.question: Should I include AI workflow on a digital marketing manager CV?
faq.answer: Yes — and it is the 2026 differentiator at every level. Name the workflow, the tool stack and the FTE-equivalent productivity gain or dollar impact. Weak: 'Familiar with ChatGPT and Jasper.' Strong (content): 'Built a Claude + Surfer + Webflow content engine producing 40 SEO-optimised long-form pages monthly (≈1.5 FTE equivalent), generating 14K incremental organic sessions and 38 SQLs/quarter at $0 incremental ad spend.' Strong (ABM): 'Operated a Clay + Apollo + GPT-4 enrichment pipeline qualifying 1,800 net-new accounts/month against the ICP at 22% reply-rate on outbound — replaced ~$11K/month in outsourced research.' Strong (creative): 'Shipped a Midjourney + Runway + Meta Advantage+ creative pipeline producing 60 ad variants/week — lifted ad-creative ROAS 23% by feeding the algorithm faster than agency cadence allowed.'

faq.question: How do I write a digital marketing manager bullet that passes ATS and recruiter screens?
faq.answer: Use the channel + volume + outcome pattern: open with a strong verb (Owned, Scaled, Shipped, Lifted, Reduced, Built), state the channel and unit of work, state the volume ($1.4M quarterly Meta budget, 412 SQLs, 32 experiments, 84K monthly organic sessions), close with the business outcome (4.6× ROAS, $2,910 CAC, $5.2M influenced pipeline, +$840K ARR run-rate, 23% activation lift). Avoid 'managed', 'helped', 'oversaw' as opening verbs. Include at least one named platform per bullet (HubSpot, Meta CAPI, GA4, LinkedIn, Klaviyo) so the bullet survives boolean recruiter search. Quote the test confidence (95% CI, n=48,000) where available — it reads as senior.

faq.question: What salary should a Senior Digital Marketing Manager target in 2026?
faq.answer: US base: $145,000–$190,000 at well-funded Series B-D B2B SaaS; +$25,000–$70,000 in NYC, SF, LA, Seattle; +$50,000–$120,000 at FAANG-tier; bonus 15-25% target; equity 0.02-0.10% at Series C+ depending on stage and ARR. UK: £85,000–£120,000+ at SaaS scale-ups; +20-35% at LSE-listed or US-HQ subsidiaries. DTC and e-commerce premiums for named iROAS / MMM credibility (Triple Whale / Northbeam / Polar literacy adds 8-15%). Equity vs cash trade-offs: prefer equity at Series A/B if ARR > $5M; prefer cash at pre-PMF or post-PE-buyout.

title: Python Developer Resume Guide
metaDescription: How to write a Python developer resume that passes ATS screening. Key skills, resume keywords, salary data, and examples for Python developer roles in 2026.
intro: Python developers are in high demand across web development, data engineering, automation, and AI/ML. Whether you are building APIs with FastAPI, data pipelines with pandas, or production systems with Django, your resume needs to clearly show what you built, at what scale, and what impact it had. This guide covers the skills, keywords, and strategies to make your Python developer resume stand out.
responsibilities: Design and develop Python applications, APIs, and microservices for production environments
Build and maintain data processing pipelines, ETL workflows, and automation scripts
Write clean, testable, well-documented code following team coding standards
Collaborate with cross-functional teams including product, data science, and DevOps
Participate in code reviews, architecture discussions, and technical planning
Debug and optimize application performance, memory usage, and response times
Integrate third-party APIs and services into existing systems
Contribute to CI/CD pipelines and deployment automation
requiredSkills: Python 3.x with strong understanding of data structures, OOP, and async programming
Web frameworks: Django, Flask, or FastAPI
Database experience: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, or Redis
API design: RESTful APIs, GraphQL, or gRPC
Testing: pytest, unittest, or integration testing frameworks
Version control with Git and collaborative development workflows
Linux/Unix command line and shell scripting
Familiarity with cloud services (AWS, Azure, or GCP)
salaryRange: ,000 - ,000 (varies by seniority, location, and specialization)
body:
Python developer job descriptions vary widely — from web backend roles to data engineering to DevOps automation. Your resume should be tailored to the specific flavor of Python role you are targeting.

For web-focused roles, emphasize frameworks (Django, Flask, FastAPI), API design, database optimization, and production deployment experience. For data engineering roles, highlight ETL pipelines, large-scale data processing, and tools like Apache Airflow or Spark. For automation roles, show scripting productivity gains and system integration.

Quantify everything: requests per second, data volume processed, time saved through automation, team size, uptime percentages. Python is ubiquitous — what differentiates your resume is what you accomplished with it.

WadeCV can help you tailor your Python developer resume to match specific job descriptions, ensuring the right keywords and experience are highlighted for each application.
commonMistakes: Listing Python as a skill without showing applications you built with it
Ignoring the specific Python domain (web, data, ML, automation) the role requires
Missing key framework keywords that ATS systems scan for
Writing generic developer bullets that could apply to any programming language
interviewTips: Be ready to discuss Python-specific concepts: generators, decorators, context managers, GIL limitations
Prepare to explain your architecture decisions — why FastAPI over Django, why async over sync
Practice coding problems that test Pythonic solutions, not just correct solutions
Know your deployment story: how your code gets from local to production

faq.question: What Python skills should I list on my resume?
faq.answer: List the Python-specific tools and frameworks relevant to the job: Django/Flask/FastAPI for web, pandas/NumPy for data, pytest for testing, and deployment tools like Docker. Avoid listing basic syntax — show application-level skills instead.

faq.question: Is Python enough to get a developer job?
faq.answer: Python alone is a strong foundation, but most roles also expect database skills (SQL), version control (Git), API design, and some cloud platform experience. The specific extras depend on whether you target web, data, or ML roles.

faq.question: How do I show Python experience without professional work history?
faq.answer: Highlight open-source contributions, personal projects with measurable outcomes, or freelance work. Include GitHub links and describe projects using the same bullet format as professional experience — what you built, the tools you used, and the result.

title: Data Analyst Resume Guide
metaDescription: How to write a data analyst resume that passes ATS screening. Key skills, tools, resume keywords, and examples for data analyst roles across industries in 2026.
intro: Data analysts turn raw data into actionable insights that drive business decisions. Whether you work in finance, marketing, operations, or product, employers want analysts who can clean messy datasets, build dashboards, and tell a clear story with numbers. This guide covers the skills, keywords, and strategies to make your data analyst resume stand out across industries.
responsibilities: Collect, clean, and transform data from multiple sources for analysis
Build dashboards and reports using BI tools like Tableau, Power BI, or Looker
Write SQL queries to extract and aggregate data from relational databases
Perform exploratory data analysis to identify trends, patterns, and anomalies
Collaborate with stakeholders to define KPIs and translate business questions into data tasks
Create and maintain data documentation, data dictionaries, and process guides
Conduct A/B test analysis and statistical significance testing
Present findings to non-technical audiences using clear visualizations and summaries
requiredSkills: SQL proficiency including joins, subqueries, window functions, and CTEs
Excel or Google Sheets including pivot tables, VLOOKUP, and conditional formatting
BI and visualization tools: Tableau, Power BI, Looker, or Metabase
Python or R for data analysis: pandas, NumPy, matplotlib, or ggplot2
Statistical concepts: hypothesis testing, regression analysis, probability distributions
Data cleaning and transformation (ETL fundamentals)
Communication skills to present data findings to business stakeholders
Familiarity with data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift) and cloud platforms
salaryRange: $55,000 - $110,000 (varies by industry, location, and experience level)
careerPath: Director of Analytics / Head of Data
body:
Data analyst job descriptions vary by industry, but the core skills overlap heavily: SQL, a BI tool, basic statistics, and the ability to communicate findings clearly. Your resume should show what questions you answered, what tools you used, and what business impact your analysis had.

For analyst roles in tech and product teams, emphasize experiment design, A/B testing, and product metrics (DAU, retention, conversion). For finance and operations roles, highlight forecasting, cost analysis, and process optimization. Marketing analytics roles want attribution modeling, campaign performance, and customer segmentation.

Quantify results wherever possible: revenue influenced, cost savings identified, time reduced through automation, dashboard adoption rates. Hiring managers want to see that your analysis led to decisions, not just that you produced reports.

Avoid listing tools without context. Instead of writing 'SQL' alone, show what you did with it: 'Built SQL queries processing 50M+ rows daily to generate executive revenue dashboards.' Every bullet should demonstrate tool plus outcome.

WadeCV can help you tailor your data analyst resume to match specific job descriptions, ensuring the right technical skills and industry keywords are highlighted for each application.
commonMistakes: Listing tools without showing what you accomplished with them
Writing generic bullets like 'analyzed data' without specifying what data or what outcome
Omitting business impact — reports and dashboards are outputs, not results
Ignoring the industry context of the role (healthcare vs fintech vs e-commerce analysis differ)
interviewTips: Prepare a portfolio piece: a dashboard or analysis you can walk through end-to-end
Practice SQL live — expect window functions, CTEs, and joins on whiteboard or online IDE
Be ready to explain statistical concepts in plain language to a non-technical interviewer
Know your metrics story: for every analysis on your resume, know what decision it informed

faq.question: What skills should a data analyst put on their resume?
faq.answer: Lead with SQL and your primary BI tool (Tableau, Power BI, or Looker). Add Python or R if you use them for analysis. Include Excel if the role mentions it. Then list domain-specific skills like A/B testing, forecasting, or customer segmentation based on the job description.

faq.question: How do I transition into a data analyst role?
faq.answer: Build a portfolio with 2-3 projects using real or public datasets. Get comfortable with SQL and one BI tool. Complete a certificate (Google Data Analytics, IBM Data Analyst) for credibility. On your resume, reframe past work to highlight data-adjacent tasks like reporting, metrics tracking, or process analysis.

faq.question: Should I list every tool I know on my data analyst resume?
faq.answer: No. List tools that match the job description and that you can discuss confidently in an interview. A focused list of 8-10 tools you actually use is better than a list of 25 tools you touched once. Prioritize SQL, your BI tool, and any programming languages the role requires.
